pick.rationale: Texas Tech holds a commanding 45-50 spot advantage in advanced metrics with a +25.19 adjusted efficiency margin that nearly doubles Akron's +13.02 mark, driven by an elite top-10 offense that presents a severe mismatch against the Zips' 99th-ranked defense. However, this is a live 5/12 upset scenario: Texas Tech has lost three straight games entering the tournament and is missing star forward JT Toppin to a season-ending ACL tear, significantly weakening their frontcourt. Akron enters with exceptional momentum, having won 10 straight games and three consecutive MAC championships behind clutch performers like Shammah Scott, who hit the game-winning three-pointer in the MAC title game. While the Red Raiders' statistical superiority and Big 12-tested pedigree provide a narrow path to survive, the Zips' combination of experience, momentum, and Texas Tech's injury-driven slump makes this one of the most vulnerable 5-seeds in the bracket.

reasoningStep.title: Texas Tech vs Akron 5/12 Upset Assessment
reasoningStep.summary: Analyzed a volatile 5/12 matchup where Texas Tech's elite statistical profile (top-15 offense, +25 EM) conflicts with their three-game losing streak and season-ending ACL injury to star JT Toppin. Akron brings historic program momentum with 29 wins, three straight MAC championships, and a 10-game winning streak, creating a classic upset formula. Despite the compelling narrative for Akron, Texas Tech's 50-spot ranking advantage and efficient offense provide sufficient margin to narrowly favor the favorite with reduced confidence.

result.text: Is Texas Tech basketball's pre-March Madness losing skid troubling? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers With 3 straight losses entering March Madness, is Texas Tech in trouble? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Nobody is going to pity Grant McCasland. He's handsomely paid to be the head coach of the Texas Tech basketball team at one of the most ambitious spending athletic departments in the country. But at the same time, you've gotta feel for the guy. McCasland has been the head coach at Tech for three full years now, spanning 103 total games . In that time, he's had his full arsenal available to him in just 16 games. Injuries have become second nature to McCasland that it seems he doesn't even flinch when another pops up. Half your rotation out for most of the year? Cool, let's set the program record for wins over Top 10 teams. Lose a national player of the year, Big 12 player of the year, and first team All-American candidate in one full swoop? Big whoop, we'll go into Ames and beat Iowa State. McCasland is an eternal optimist, always able to flip the negative into something deeper. It's part of what makes him such a great coach: finding joy in the struggle to reach the top of his profession. Even McCasland, though, has to think something's gotta give at some point. If it's not JT Toppin, having a historic season, tearing his ACL so close to the end of the season — after his team's bus tires were slashed after beating Arizona — it's seeing his other All-American, Christian Anderson, slipping and sliding all over the Big 12's insane ASP GlassFloor LED court , causing he and LeJuan Watts (perhaps an unrelated injury, perhaps related) to exit their Big 12 Tournament game with ailments. Compounding the current injury factor is the previous injuries to guys like Josiah Moseley, Luke Bamgboye and Marial Akuentok that set the team back from jump street. Before the season could even begin, the Red Raiders were shorthanded. It's the story of McCasland's existence at Texas Tech. Now is not the time to play the sympathy card, not that McCasland ever would. But he is a realist. As much as he wants to look at the bright side of things, he knows the Red Raiders and trending in the wrong direction and the worst possible time. Three straight losses to NCAA Tournament-bound teams isn't the end of the world. How they came about and what it says about his team's current state could be. The team has lacked poise under pressure, second-guessing every move th

result.text: Akron will take third straight trip to March Madness as MAC champion Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Shammah Scott's 3 lifts Akron to MAC Men's Basketball Tournament title Michael Beaven Akron Beacon Journal CLEVELAND — The University of Akron men's basketball team found another way to win a Mid-American Conference Tournament championship in dramatic fashion for the third year in a row Saturday, March 14 at Rocket Arena. Akron graduate student Shammah Scott joined former Zips standouts Greg Tribble Jr. and Nate Johnson in making a last-second game-winning shot to clinch a MAC Tournament title and a spot in the NCAA Tournament. Scott swished a 3-pointer from about NBA range with 6.0 seconds remaining to lift No. 2 seed Akron to a 79-76 win over No. 4 seed Toledo before a crowd of 11,072. Scott's 3 to beat Toledo in the 2026 MAC Tournament final came after Johnson made a floating layup with 2.3 seconds remaining to give Akron a 76-74 win over Miami in the 2025 final and Tribble made two free throws with 4.8 seconds left to lift Akron to a 62-61 win over Kent State in the 2024 final. “I knew I had to make a play,” said Scott, a 6-foot-2 guard and a two-time MAC Sixth Man of the Year . “Going into the huddle, the coaches put a lot of trust in me, so I just wanted to come through for my coaches and my teammates. I love all these dudes and I wouldn't want to win with nobody else. “… It's a very cool moment. This is definitely something that I dreamed of, and I'm just blessed and grateful to be put in this situation. Words can’t explain what I'm feeling right now.” Toledo (19-15) almost forced overtime when Rockets junior center Austin Parks attempted an off-balance desperation 3-pointer that hit off the back of the rim as time expired. MAC Freshman of the Year Leroy Blyden Jr. dribbled the ball up the court after Scott's 3, but lost control of it when Akron senior Evan Mahaffey poked it toward's Toledo's bench, where Parks was standing. "I'm tired," Akron coach John Groce said. "Unbelievable night. ... It's such a privilege to coach those guys. “We wanted to get the ball in his [Scott's] hands there and make sure that we, as much as we could there was a little bit of a differential in game clock and shot clock, but we wanted to give ourselves the best chance to have the last shot. With the ball in his hands, obviously we trust him, his teammates trust him, and he made a big shot.” Akron (29-5) is the first team to earn a three-peat as the champion of the MAC Men's Basketball Tournament since the event started in 1980. Toledo won the first MAC Tournament title and is still searching for its second crown. “It's unfathomable,” Groce said of the three-peat. “You think about all the great coaches and great players that have been in this league, the ones that are currently in the league, and how long the league's been around. It's humbling.”

result.text: Texas Tech vs Akron prediction, pick for March Madness first round Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Big Ten Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Texas Tech basketball vs Akron prediction | Our pick for 2026 NCAA bracket first round Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal What does the 2026 NCAA Tournament have in store for the Texas Tech basketball team? It begins Friday in Tampa when the 5th-seeded Red Raiders (22-10) take on the 12-seed Akron Zips (29-5), the tournament champions out of the MAC. The Zips have won 10 in a row and 19 of their last 20 games (their only loss in MAC play came against Miami (Ohio) as they ran the gauntlet for an undefeated regular season), while Texas Tech enters March Madness after losing three straight games. How do the Red Raiders and the Zips stack up? Here's a look at the matchup and a score prediction. Texas Tech basketball vs Akron in March Madness: Players to watch Christian Anderson, Texas Tech Christian Anderson has turned himself into a projected first-round pick in the 2026 NBA Draft thanks to his lethal shooting ability (42.5% from 3-point range) and playmaking ability (7.6 assists per game). He's had to navigate life after the season-ending injury to JT Toppin but Anderson can still fill up the box score, having put the team on his back with a 27-point effort in the win over Duke earlier this year. Anderson was a first-team All-Big 12 selection and a third-team All-American by The Sporting News, one of four outlets that determine consensus All-American status. Tavari Johnson, Akron Akron had two first-team all-MAC selections with one of them being senior guard Tavari Johnson. The 6-footer made a big leap in the scoring department this season, climbing his season average to 20.1 points per game during the 2025-26 season while shooting 37% from 3 and over 80% from the free throw line. He also leads the team in assists (170) and is second in steals (43). Texas Tech basketball vs Akron in March Madness: Stats to know Akron ranks eighth in the country averaging 88.6 points per game while the Red Raiders have struggled on the defensive end since Toppin went down for the year. Both teams are high-volume 3-point shooting squads. Texas Tech is sixth in the nation in 3-point percentage (39.34%) while Akron isn't far being (38.20%) ranking 18th in the country. There is a common opponent between the two teams, each having lost to Purdue this season. Akron lost 97-79 in West Lafayette while the Red Raiders were clubbed 86-56 in the Bahamas by the Boilermakers. Score prediction for Texas Tech basketball vs Akron in NCAA Tournament Akron 84, Texas Tech 80: We have to call it like it is. Akron is one of the hottest teams in the country and features an explosive offense while Texas Tech hasn't had the same level of intensity on the defensive end during its three-game losing streak. This could be an early exit for the Red Raiders. Facebook Twitter Email Help Terms of Service Su

result.text: Is Texas Tech basketball's pre-March Madness losing skid troubling? Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers With 3 straight losses entering March Madness, is Texas Tech in trouble? Nathan Giese Lubbock Avalanche-Journal KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Nobody is going to pity Grant McCasland. He's handsomely paid to be the head coach of the Texas Tech basketball team at one of the most ambitious spending athletic departments in the country. But at the same time, you've gotta feel for the guy. McCasland has been the head coach at Tech for three full years now, spanning 103 total games . In that time, he's had his full arsenal available to him in just 16 games. Injuries have become second nature to McCasland that it seems he doesn't even flinch when another pops up. Half your rotation out for most of the year? Cool, let's set the program record for wins over Top 10 teams. Lose a national player of the year, Big 12 player of the year, and first team All-American candidate in one full swoop? Big whoop, we'll go into Ames and beat Iowa State. McCasland is an eternal optimist, always able to flip the negative into something deeper. It's part of what makes him such a great coach: finding joy in the struggle to reach the top of his profession. Even McCasland, though, has to think something's gotta give at some point. If it's not JT Toppin, having a historic season, tearing his ACL so close to the end of the season — after his team's bus tires were slashed after beating Arizona — it's seeing his other All-American, Christian Anderson, slipping and sliding all over the Big 12's insane ASP GlassFloor LED court , causing he and LeJuan Watts (perhaps an unrelated injury, perhaps related) to exit their Big 12 Tournament game with ailments. Compounding the current injury factor is the previous injuries to guys like Josiah Moseley, Luke Bamgboye and Marial Akuentok that set the team back from jump street. Before the season could even begin, the Red Raiders were shorthanded. It's the story of McCasland's existence at Texas Tech. Now is not the time to play the sympathy card, not that McCasland ever would. But he is a realist. As much as he wants to look at the bright side of things, he knows the Red Raiders and trending in the wrong direction and the worst possible time. Three straight losses to NCAA Tournament-bound teams isn't the end of the world. How they came about and what it says about his team's current state could be. The team has lacked poise under pressure, second-guessing every move th

Akron will take third straight trip to March Madness as MAC champion Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Shammah Scott's 3 lifts Akron to MAC Men's Basketball Tournament title Michael Beaven Akron Beacon Journal CLEVELAND — The University of Akron men's basketball team found another way to win a Mid-American Conference Tournament championship in dramatic fashion for the third year in a row Saturday, March 14 at Rocket Arena. Akron graduate student Shammah Scott joined former Zips standouts Greg Tribble Jr. and Nate Johnson in making a last-second game-winning shot to clinch a MAC Tournament title and a spot in the NCAA Tournament. Scott swished a 3-pointer from about NBA range with 6.0 seconds remaining to lift No. 2 seed Akron to a 79-76 win over No. 4 seed Toledo before a crowd of 11,072. Scott's 3 to beat Toledo in the 2026 MAC Tournament final came after Johnson made a floating layup with 2.3 seconds remaining to give Akron a 76-74 win over Miami in the 2025 final and Tribble made two free throws with 4.8 seconds left to lift Akron to a 62-61 win over Kent State in the 2024 final. “I knew I had to make a play,” said Scott, a 6-foot-2 guard and a two-time MAC Sixth Man of the Year . “Going into the huddle, the coaches put a lot of trust in me, so I just wanted to come through for my coaches and my teammates. I love all these dudes and I wouldn't want to win with nobody else. “… It's a very cool moment. This is definitely something that I dreamed of, and I'm just blessed and grateful to be put in this situation. Words can’t explain what I'm feeling right now.” Toledo (19-15) almost forced overtime when Rockets junior center Austin Parks attempted an off-balance desperation 3-pointer that hit off the back of the rim as time expired. MAC Freshman of the Year Leroy Blyden Jr. dribbled the ball up the court after Scott's 3, but lost control of it when Akron senior Evan Mahaffey poked it toward's Toledo's bench, where Parks was standing. "I'm tired," Akron coach John Groce said. "Unbelievable night. ... It's such a privilege to coach those guys. “We wanted to get the ball in his [Scott's] hands there and make sure that we, as much as we could there was a little bit of a differential in game clock and shot clock, but we wanted to give ourselves the best chance to have the last shot. With the ball in his hands, obviously we trust him, his teammates trust him, and he made a big shot.” Akron (29-5) is the first team to earn a three-peat as the champion of the MAC Men's Basketball Tournament since the event started in 1980. Toledo won the first MAC Tournament title and is still searching for its second crown. “It's unfathomable,” Groce said of the three-peat. “You think about all the great coaches and great players that have been in this league, the ones that are currently in the league, and how long the league's been around. It's humbling.”
